input.instructions: Build a Trie from the given N bitstrings. For each node in the Trie, track whether it represents the end of one of the original IDs. Identify all nodes that are 'terminal' (the end of an original ID). For each terminal node, determine if it is an ancestor of another terminal node in the original set. This identifies the initial conflicts.

variables.selected_prompt_text:
Solve the question directly.
Keep the entire JSON compact.
Keep decision_notes extremely short. Prefer 3 to 8 words.
Do not narrate your reasoning outside the response field.
In the response field, use exactly two short lines encoded with \n:
FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>
Keep the justification to 12 words or fewer.
Use plain text only inside the response field. Do not use markdown formatting, code fences, or math-style backslashes such as \equiv.
Do not add any extra text before or after the JSON object.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "execute_locally",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "response": "FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>\nJUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>",
  "result_type": "summary"
}

Question:
Build a Trie from the given N bitstrings. For each node in the Trie, track whether it represents the end of one of the original IDs. Identify all nodes that are 'terminal' (the end of an original ID). For each terminal node, determine if it is an ancestor of another terminal node in the original set. This identifies the initial conflicts.

results.content:
FINAL_ANSWER: A terminal node is a conflict if it has descendants that are also terminal nodes.
JUSTIFICATION: Terminal nodes with terminal descendants represent prefix conflicts.
